Relevance: 0.586 (consider)

The Western Shandong Province (WSP) in the North China Craton (NCC) preserves extensive Neoarchean supracrustal rocks, which are key to understanding the early tectonic evolution of the eastern NCC. This study presents an integrated petrological, mineralogical and geochronological investigation on supracrustal rocks from the Taishan Group in the WSP. Phase equilibrium modeling and geothermobarometry results reveal that these rocks record consistent clockwise P–T paths. They experienced peak amphibolite-facies metamorphism under low- to medium-pressure conditions (∼4.5–5.7 kbar/600–700°C), followed by a retrograde stage characterized by near-isothermal decompression. LA-ICP-MS U-Pb dating of monazite constrains the timing of this metamorphic event to ∼ 2.53–2.50 Ga. Detrital zircon ages of ∼ 2.75 Ga and ∼ 2.55 Ga in a metapelite sample indicate provenance from both early and late Neoarchean crust and constrain the maximum depositional age of its sedimentary protolith to ∼ 2.55 Ga. The absence of medium- to high-pressure facies series metamorphism and the distinct clockwise P–T paths (compared to the anticlockwise paths reported for the high-grade Yishui Group) suggest that the ∼ 2.5 Ga tectonic regime in the WSP might not have been a simple continuation of an earlier mantle plume or a typical subduction-collision setting. Combined with regional data, this study favors a geodynamic model involving the amalgamation of micro-continents during the late Neoarchean in the WSP.

item.title: Serpentinization parageneses controlled by lithological variations among olivine gabbro and peridotite from the Atlantis Massif, Mid-Atlantic Ridge
item.dc:creator: Toshio Nozaka
Shun Kazumata
Frieder Klein
item.description: Serpentinization of olivine impacts the geochemical and ecological environment via molecular hydrogen production, promoted by iron oxidation to form magnetite and ferric iron in serpentine. Previous studies suggested that serpentinization proceeds with various reactions and the extent of iron oxidation is variable depending on the reaction pathways. To examine the effect of lithological variation on reactions at an incipient stage of serpentinization, we carried out microscopic observations, electron probe analyses, and Raman spectroscopy of serpentine veins cutting olivine in gabbroic rocks and peridotites from the Atlantis Massif, Mid-Atlantic Ridge. The results indicate that the serpentine veins in the proximity of olivine dominantly comprise lizardite mixed with variable minor phases or components depending on igneous lithology: brucite in peridotites, troctolites, and primitive olivine gabbros; cronstedtite-bearing serpentine, commonly without brucite, in some troctolites and slightly evolved olivine gabbros; and neither brucite nor cronstedtite component in evolved olivine gabbros. This suggests that incipient-stage serpentinization paragenesis is dominantly controlled by silica activity reflecting lithological variation. In addition to magnetite, the formation of cronstedtite in gabbroic rocks has the potential to produce hydrogen. The presence or absence of brucite and cronstedtite could affect the timing and magnitude of iron oxidation and hydrogen production during serpentinization. Considering the predominance of gabbroic rocks in the lower oceanic crust, the variation in incipient-stage serpentinization paragenesis in gabbroic rocks may have an impact on the redox state and ecological environment around the (sub)seafloor. Published: 2026-07-17T23:43:38+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.581 (consider)

DOI: 10.1016/j.lithos.2026.108716

Relevance: 0.581 (consider)

Serpentinization of olivine impacts the geochemical and ecological environment via molecular hydrogen production, promoted by iron oxidation to form magnetite and ferric iron in serpentine. Previous studies suggested that serpentinization proceeds with various reactions and the extent of iron oxidation is variable depending on the reaction pathways. To examine the effect of lithological variation on reactions at an incipient stage of serpentinization, we carried out microscopic observations, electron probe analyses, and Raman spectroscopy of serpentine veins cutting olivine in gabbroic rocks and peridotites from the Atlantis Massif, Mid-Atlantic Ridge. The results indicate that the serpentine veins in the proximity of olivine dominantly comprise lizardite mixed with variable minor phases or components depending on igneous lithology: brucite in peridotites, troctolites, and primitive olivine gabbros; cronstedtite-bearing serpentine, commonly without brucite, in some troctolites and slightly evolved olivine gabbros; and neither brucite nor cronstedtite component in evolved olivine gabbros. This suggests that incipient-stage serpentinization paragenesis is dominantly controlled by silica activity reflecting lithological variation. In addition to magnetite, the formation of cronstedtite in gabbroic rocks has the potential to produce hydrogen. The presence or absence of brucite and cronstedtite could affect the timing and magnitude of iron oxidation and hydrogen production during serpentinization. Considering the predominance of gabbroic rocks in the lower oceanic crust, the variation in incipient-stage serpentinization paragenesis in gabbroic rocks may have an impact on the redox state and ecological environment around the (sub)seafloor.

item.title: The Complexity of Deep Aqueous Fluids in Planetary Ultramafic Mantles
item.dc:creator: Sophia E. Economon
Dimitri A. Sverjensky
item.description: Abstract Planetary volatiles cycle through a planet's crust, mantle, and atmosphere, playing a crucial role in surface processes and habitability. Discussions of planetary volatiles traditionally focus on water, carbon, nitrogen, and sulfur, but fluids in contact with silicate rocks and ultramafic mantles can contain significant amounts of magnesium and silicon. Ultramafic mantles may be common in our solar system and can be expected in many exoplanets. These environments have been studied through fluid inclusions in diamonds from Earth's upper mantle and in experiments of chloride‐free fluids in equilibrium with peridotite. It appears that magnesium‐chloride complexing is increasingly relevant at high temperatures and pressures, but such complexes are largely unconstrained for deep conditions. Effective models are needed to understanding volatile cycling on terrestrial worlds in our solar system and beyond. To address this, we analyzed published experimental data for silicate mineral solubilities in chloride‐rich fluids to assess fluid speciation in ultramafic planetary mantles. We regressed experimental solubilities of talc + quartz and tremolite + talc + quartz at 2 kb and 500–700°C, as well as solubilities of forsterite at 10 kb and 800–900°C to obtain new equilibrium constants for and revised equilibrium constants for several magnesium‐, calcium‐, and silicon‐bearing species. We then applied the DEW model to characterize the equilibrium constants over wide ranges of temperature and pressure. As a result, we produce a new and robust thermodynamic data set for magnesium‐, calcium‐, and silicon‐chloride complexes at upper mantle conditions which enable modeling of magnesium transport in deep planetary fluids. Published: 2026-07-18T19:00:46+00:00 Venue: Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems Score: 0.580 (consider)

Authors: Sophia E. Economon; Dimitri A. Sverjensky

Journal: Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems

DOI: 10.1029/2025gc012637

Relevance: 0.580 (consider)

Abstract Planetary volatiles cycle through a planet's crust, mantle, and atmosphere, playing a crucial role in surface processes and habitability. Discussions of planetary volatiles traditionally focus on water, carbon, nitrogen, and sulfur, but fluids in contact with silicate rocks and ultramafic mantles can contain significant amounts of magnesium and silicon. Ultramafic mantles may be common in our solar system and can be expected in many exoplanets. These environments have been studied through fluid inclusions in diamonds from Earth's upper mantle and in experiments of chloride‐free fluids in equilibrium with peridotite. It appears that magnesium‐chloride complexing is increasingly relevant at high temperatures and pressures, but such complexes are largely unconstrained for deep conditions. Effective models are needed to understanding volatile cycling on terrestrial worlds in our solar system and beyond. To address this, we analyzed published experimental data for silicate mineral solubilities in chloride‐rich fluids to assess fluid speciation in ultramafic planetary mantles. We regressed experimental solubilities of talc + quartz and tremolite + talc + quartz at 2 kb and 500–700°C, as well as solubilities of forsterite at 10 kb and 800–900°C to obtain new equilibrium constants for and revised equilibrium constants for several magnesium‐, calcium‐, and silicon‐bearing species. We then applied the DEW model to characterize the equilibrium constants over wide ranges of temperature and pressure. As a result, we produce a new and robust thermodynamic data set for magnesium‐, calcium‐, and silicon‐chloride complexes at upper mantle conditions which enable modeling of magnesium transport in deep planetary fluids.

item.title: Magmatic Fertility Indicators of the Chagai Arc: A Systematic Review of Published Whole-Rock Geochemistry and Implications for Porphyry Cu–Au Prospectivity
item.dc:creator: Khan, Mubashir
item.description: The porphyry copper–gold (Cu–Au) deposits of the world are the primary source for copper and are significant producers of gold and molybdenum, and the genesis is always linked to the geochemical characteristics of the arc magmas from which the deposits crystallised. The Chagai Arc of western Pakistan is a Neotethyan subduction-related magmatic belt with several significant porphyry prospects, namely the Reko Diq, the Saindak and the Koh-i-Sultan, which constitute one of the least-developed large porphyry Cu–Au provinces of the Tethyan metallogenic domain. The importance of the arc has not been assessed, however, as an integrated geochemical system within a fertility sensitive discrimination approach. Rock data sets already exist for each of the areas of the deposits, and these have been used and interpreted independently without a consistent criterion which makes it difficult to compare data from one area with another. Loucks, Henríquez and Fiorentini (2024, Economic Geology) proposed the whole-rock magmatic fertility framework, which is based on the ratios of Sr/Y, (La/Yb)N, Eu/Eu, V/Sc and Dy/Yb as proxies for fractionation depth and magmatic oxidation state, has not been applied to any arc segment in Pakistan. This study uses a set of validated, individually sourced published values (specifically Sr/Y and europium anomaly systematics by Richards et al. (2012) for ore-related Chagai and Iranian Tethyan intrusions, U–Pb zircon and molybdenite Re–Os geochronology by Wang et al. (2022) and Muhammad et al. (2024) for Saindak and Reko Diq, and compositional and petrogenetic data by Nicholson et al. (2010) for Koh-i-Sultan), not an independently re-compiled, sample-level multi-source database. The two rocks, Reko Diq and Saindak, have mean chondrite-normalised europium anomalies of Eu(N)/Eu = 0.88 and Sr/Y > 30, which are consistent with deep and hydrous differentiation that is preferred in the formation of porphyry Cu–Au-type ores under plagioclase-suppressed conditions (Richards et al., 2012). The evidence for the ore-forming event in the area of Saindak (~22.2 Ma) is much older than the main Reko Diq mineralising event (~11–12 Ma), suggesting that there was at least two distinct pulses of fertile magmas in this portion of the arc. Koh-i-Sultan (<2.5 Ma) is compositionally distinct (basaltic andesite to dacite, ordinary calc-alkaline trend) and has no published Sr/Y, Eu/Eu or V/Sc dataset available from this centre that could be found in this synthesis. This synthesis is the first application of the fertility discriminant scheme by Loucks et al. (2024) to a Pakistani arc system and it is concluded that the most direct path towards a fully quantitative, arc-scale map of the Chagai Belt fertility that could be used to guide porphyry exploration in the Chagai system and other similar Tethyan systems is through the re-extraction of samples from the cited datasets. Published: 2026-07-18T15:36:00+00:00 Venue: EarthArXiv Score: 0.577 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Khan, Mubashir

Journal: EarthArXiv

DOI: 10.31223/X5CZ0G

Relevance: 0.577 (consider)

The porphyry copper–gold (Cu–Au) deposits of the world are the primary source for copper and are significant producers of gold and molybdenum, and the genesis is always linked to the geochemical characteristics of the arc magmas from which the deposits crystallised. The Chagai Arc of western Pakistan is a Neotethyan subduction-related magmatic belt with several significant porphyry prospects, namely the Reko Diq, the Saindak and the Koh-i-Sultan, which constitute one of the least-developed large porphyry Cu–Au provinces of the Tethyan metallogenic domain. The importance of the arc has not been assessed, however, as an integrated geochemical system within a fertility sensitive discrimination approach. Rock data sets already exist for each of the areas of the deposits, and these have been used and interpreted independently without a consistent criterion which makes it difficult to compare data from one area with another. Loucks, Henríquez and Fiorentini (2024, Economic Geology) proposed the whole-rock magmatic fertility framework, which is based on the ratios of Sr/Y, (La/Yb)N, Eu/Eu, V/Sc and Dy/Yb as proxies for fractionation depth and magmatic oxidation state, has not been applied to any arc segment in Pakistan. This study uses a set of validated, individually sourced published values (specifically Sr/Y and europium anomaly systematics by Richards et al. (2012) for ore-related Chagai and Iranian Tethyan intrusions, U–Pb zircon and molybdenite Re–Os geochronology by Wang et al. (2022) and Muhammad et al. (2024) for Saindak and Reko Diq, and compositional and petrogenetic data by Nicholson et al. (2010) for Koh-i-Sultan), not an independently re-compiled, sample-level multi-source database. The two rocks, Reko Diq and Saindak, have mean chondrite-normalised europium anomalies of Eu(N)/Eu = 0.88 and Sr/Y > 30, which are consistent with deep and hydrous differentiation that is preferred in the formation of porphyry Cu–Au-type ores under plagioclase-suppressed conditions (Richards et al., 2012). The evidence for the ore-forming event in the area of Saindak (~22.2 Ma) is much older than the main Reko Diq mineralising event (~11–12 Ma), suggesting that there was at least two distinct pulses of fertile magmas in this portion of the arc. Koh-i-Sultan (<2.5 Ma) is compositionally distinct (basaltic andesite to dacite, ordinary calc-alkaline trend) and has no published Sr/Y, Eu/Eu or V/Sc dataset available from this centre that could be found in this synthesis. This synthesis is the first application of the fertility discriminant scheme by Loucks et al. (2024) to a Pakistani arc system and it is concluded that the most direct path towards a fully quantitative, arc-scale map of the Chagai Belt fertility that could be used to guide porphyry exploration in the Chagai system and other similar Tethyan systems is through the re-extraction of samples from the cited datasets.

item.title: Geochemical constraints on the Yangtze–Cathaysia block boundary from early paleozoic I-type granitoids across the Chenzhou–Linwu fault
item.dc:creator: Yi-Xin Liu
Xiu-Quan Miao
Yang Yu
Xiao-Long Huang
item.description: Pre-existing sutures, as the fundamental lithospheric boundaries, potentially represent lithospheric weakened zones that are easily to be reactivated and transformed, playing a crucial role in the evolution of continental blocks. In the South China Block (SCB), the Neoproterozoic suture between the Yangtze and Cathaysia blocks is well defined in the northeast along the Jiangshan–Shaoxing–Pingxiang fault (JSPF). However, whether this suture extends southward to the Chenzhou–Linwu fault (CLF) remains controversial, limiting our understanding of the tectonic evolution of the SCB. Thus, we present zircon UPb ages and HfO isotopic data, together with whole-rock geochemistry and SrNd isotopic data, for the Shangbao and Guidong granitoids located east of the CLF and the Daning granitoids situated to the west, respectively. Zircon UPb ages indicate that the studied granitoids were emplaced at ca. 443–435 Ma. All granitoids are I-type granitoids derived from the partial melting of ancient igneous basement. The high Y + Nb contents (144–593 ppm) of these granitoids, a planar distribution of Early Paleozoic granites in the SCB, and the absence of subduction-related records collectively support an intracontinental orogen for the Wuyi-Yunkai orogen (WYO). Furthermore, the Shangbao and Guidong granitoids, together with western Cathaysia I-type granitoids, exhibit lower Pb, Th, and U concentrations, and more negative εNd(t) and εHf(t) values than the Daning granitoids and eastern Yangtze I-type granitoids. This highlights a compositional contrast in crustal basements between the eastern and western segments of the CLF, characterized by minimal juvenile input in the former and a juvenile materials-modified basement in the latter. These findings suggest that the boundary between the Yangtze and Cathaysia blocks extends southward to the CLF, providing insights into the orogenic processes of the WYO, and the role of weak lithospheric boundaries in controlling asthenospheric upwelling and emplacement of mantle-derived magmas. Published: 2026-07-16T05:29:54+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.575 (consider)

Authors: Yi-Xin Liu; Xiu-Quan Miao; Yang Yu; Xiao-Long Huang

Journal: Lithos

DOI: 10.1016/j.lithos.2026.108711

Relevance: 0.575 (consider)

Pre-existing sutures, as the fundamental lithospheric boundaries, potentially represent lithospheric weakened zones that are easily to be reactivated and transformed, playing a crucial role in the evolution of continental blocks. In the South China Block (SCB), the Neoproterozoic suture between the Yangtze and Cathaysia blocks is well defined in the northeast along the Jiangshan–Shaoxing–Pingxiang fault (JSPF). However, whether this suture extends southward to the Chenzhou–Linwu fault (CLF) remains controversial, limiting our understanding of the tectonic evolution of the SCB. Thus, we present zircon UPb ages and HfO isotopic data, together with whole-rock geochemistry and SrNd isotopic data, for the Shangbao and Guidong granitoids located east of the CLF and the Daning granitoids situated to the west, respectively. Zircon UPb ages indicate that the studied granitoids were emplaced at ca. 443–435 Ma. All granitoids are I-type granitoids derived from the partial melting of ancient igneous basement. The high Y + Nb contents (144–593 ppm) of these granitoids, a planar distribution of Early Paleozoic granites in the SCB, and the absence of subduction-related records collectively support an intracontinental orogen for the Wuyi-Yunkai orogen (WYO). Furthermore, the Shangbao and Guidong granitoids, together with western Cathaysia I-type granitoids, exhibit lower Pb, Th, and U concentrations, and more negative εNd(t) and εHf(t) values than the Daning granitoids and eastern Yangtze I-type granitoids. This highlights a compositional contrast in crustal basements between the eastern and western segments of the CLF, characterized by minimal juvenile input in the former and a juvenile materials-modified basement in the latter. These findings suggest that the boundary between the Yangtze and Cathaysia blocks extends southward to the CLF, providing insights into the orogenic processes of the WYO, and the role of weak lithospheric boundaries in controlling asthenospheric upwelling and emplacement of mantle-derived magmas.

item.title: Petrogenesis of the middle Jurassic Luorong syenite and Xishan A-type granite in the southern Qin-Hang belt, South China: Implications for contrasting mixed sources and divergent differentiation pathways
item.dc:creator: Zheng-Wei Qin
Xi-Yao Li
Wolfgang Siebel
Jian-Ming Fu
Sanzhong Li
You-Yue Lu
Yu-Xiao Yao
Yu-Shuai Yu
Bo Bao
item.description: The Middle Jurassic was marked by significant magmatic activity in the interior of South China. This study presents integrated zircon and baddeleyite U–Pb geochronology, Hf–O isotopes, whole-rock geochemistry, and Sr–Nd isotopic data for two Middle Jurassic magmatic units in the southern Qin–Hang belt within the interior of the South China Block. Zircon and baddeleyite U–Pb dating yielded crystallization ages of 167–162 Ma for the Luorong Pluton and 163–162 Ma for the Xishan Pluton. The Luorong syenites exhibit moderate SiO2, high total alkali, and low MgO and Fe2O3 T, along with baddeleyite εHf(t) values of + 1.3 to + 7.8 and mantle-like zircon δ18O values (5.11–6.54 ‰). Their trace element characteristics resemble those of Jurassic shoshonitic rocks in the southern Qin–Hang belt. The Xishan granites show A-type affinities with high SiO2 contents, low MgO and Fe2O3 T contents, and negative Nb, Ta, Sr, and Eu anomalies. They display evolved whole-rock (87Sr/86Sr)i ratios (0.70562–0.70632), εNd(t) values (−0.9 to + 0.4), zircon εHf(t) values (+3.4 to + 6.4) and δ18O values (7.16–8.06 ‰); a mafic microgranular enclave from the Xishan Pluton yields corresponding values of 0.70494, +1.9, +3.4 to + 6.4, and 6.94–8.66 ‰, respectively. The Luorong syenites originated from a mixed source involving both asthenospheric and metasomatized lithospheric mantle components, followed by fractional crystallization. The Xishan A-type granites record a mixed mantle–crustal origin, involving magma mingling, as evidenced by the enclaves, followed by fractional crystallization. These contrasting differentiation pathways reflect spatially heterogeneous lithospheric extension and asthenospheric upwelling beneath the interior of the South China Block during the Middle Jurassic. Published: 2026-07-21T23:29:55+00:00 Venue: Journal of Asian Earth Sciences Score: 0.569 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zheng-Wei Qin; Xi-Yao Li; Wolfgang Siebel; Jian-Ming Fu; Sanzhong Li; You-Yue Lu; Yu-Xiao Yao; Yu-Shuai Yu; Bo Bao

Journal: Journal of Asian Earth Sciences

DOI: 10.1016/j.jseaes.2026.107233

Relevance: 0.569 (consider)

The Middle Jurassic was marked by significant magmatic activity in the interior of South China. This study presents integrated zircon and baddeleyite U–Pb geochronology, Hf–O isotopes, whole-rock geochemistry, and Sr–Nd isotopic data for two Middle Jurassic magmatic units in the southern Qin–Hang belt within the interior of the South China Block. Zircon and baddeleyite U–Pb dating yielded crystallization ages of 167–162 Ma for the Luorong Pluton and 163–162 Ma for the Xishan Pluton. The Luorong syenites exhibit moderate SiO2, high total alkali, and low MgO and Fe2O3 T, along with baddeleyite εHf(t) values of + 1.3 to + 7.8 and mantle-like zircon δ18O values (5.11–6.54 ‰). Their trace element characteristics resemble those of Jurassic shoshonitic rocks in the southern Qin–Hang belt. The Xishan granites show A-type affinities with high SiO2 contents, low MgO and Fe2O3 T contents, and negative Nb, Ta, Sr, and Eu anomalies. They display evolved whole-rock (87Sr/86Sr)i ratios (0.70562–0.70632), εNd(t) values (−0.9 to + 0.4), zircon εHf(t) values (+3.4 to + 6.4) and δ18O values (7.16–8.06 ‰); a mafic microgranular enclave from the Xishan Pluton yields corresponding values of 0.70494, +1.9, +3.4 to + 6.4, and 6.94–8.66 ‰, respectively. The Luorong syenites originated from a mixed source involving both asthenospheric and metasomatized lithospheric mantle components, followed by fractional crystallization. The Xishan A-type granites record a mixed mantle–crustal origin, involving magma mingling, as evidenced by the enclaves, followed by fractional crystallization. These contrasting differentiation pathways reflect spatially heterogeneous lithospheric extension and asthenospheric upwelling beneath the interior of the South China Block during the Middle Jurassic.

item.title: Organic matter enrichment dynamics in North China Block: Implications for carbon cycle perturbations during the late Paleozoic Ice Age deglaciation
item.dc:creator: Yanan Li
Jiao Fang
Shuai Wang
Zhaorui Ye
Xiongxiong Li
item.description: The mechanisms governing carbon cycle feedbacks during the demise of the Late Paleozoic Ice Age (LPIA) remain poorly constrained, particularly the role of regional carbon sinks transitioning to sources. The North China Block (NCB), once a major late Paleozoic carbon sink, experienced a dramatic decline in carbon sequestration during the Sakmarian–Artinskian, coincident with global deglaciation. However, the drivers of this regional shift and its potential contribution to the LPIA termination are poorly constrained. To address this, an integrated petrological, sedimentological, and geochemical study was conducted on mudstones from the Dacheng coalfield, Hebei Province, establishing an organic matter (OM) enrichment model for the coastal plain to alluvial plain transition. Results indicate that Sakmarian coastal plain mudstones, deposited under warm-humid conditions with enhanced nutrient supply and suboxic-anoxic waters, host higher OM. In contrast, Artinskian alluvial plain deposits, influenced by climatic aridification, reduced nutrient flux, and suboxic conditions, contain significantly lower OM, highlighting paleoclimate as the primary control. Based on comprehensive NCB borehole data, organic carbon (OC) burial amounts were quantified, revealing a drastic decline from 4053.5 Gt C (1185.2 Gt C/Myr) in the Sakmarian to 1876.2 Gt C (275.9 Gt C/Myr) in the Artinskian. This represents a net reduction of 2177.3 Gt C in organic carbon burial from the Sakmarian to the Artinskian. We propose that this large-scale regional decline in carbon burial from the NCB acted as a significant positive feedback, exacerbating early Artinskian warming. The termination of the LPIA was therefore driven by a synergy of processes, where the collapse of the NCB carbon sink, coupled with global volcanic emissions and reduced weathering, collectively forced the climate system past a critical threshold, ending Earth’s longest-lived Phanerozoic icehouse. Published: 2026-07-17T23:17:29+00:00 Venue: Geoscience Frontiers Score: 0.568 (consider)

Authors: Yanan Li; Jiao Fang; Shuai Wang; Zhaorui Ye; Xiongxiong Li

Journal: Geoscience Frontiers

DOI: 10.1016/j.gsf.2026.102412

Relevance: 0.568 (consider)

The mechanisms governing carbon cycle feedbacks during the demise of the Late Paleozoic Ice Age (LPIA) remain poorly constrained, particularly the role of regional carbon sinks transitioning to sources. The North China Block (NCB), once a major late Paleozoic carbon sink, experienced a dramatic decline in carbon sequestration during the Sakmarian–Artinskian, coincident with global deglaciation. However, the drivers of this regional shift and its potential contribution to the LPIA termination are poorly constrained. To address this, an integrated petrological, sedimentological, and geochemical study was conducted on mudstones from the Dacheng coalfield, Hebei Province, establishing an organic matter (OM) enrichment model for the coastal plain to alluvial plain transition. Results indicate that Sakmarian coastal plain mudstones, deposited under warm-humid conditions with enhanced nutrient supply and suboxic-anoxic waters, host higher OM. In contrast, Artinskian alluvial plain deposits, influenced by climatic aridification, reduced nutrient flux, and suboxic conditions, contain significantly lower OM, highlighting paleoclimate as the primary control. Based on comprehensive NCB borehole data, organic carbon (OC) burial amounts were quantified, revealing a drastic decline from 4053.5 Gt C (1185.2 Gt C/Myr) in the Sakmarian to 1876.2 Gt C (275.9 Gt C/Myr) in the Artinskian. This represents a net reduction of 2177.3 Gt C in organic carbon burial from the Sakmarian to the Artinskian. We propose that this large-scale regional decline in carbon burial from the NCB acted as a significant positive feedback, exacerbating early Artinskian warming. The termination of the LPIA was therefore driven by a synergy of processes, where the collapse of the NCB carbon sink, coupled with global volcanic emissions and reduced weathering, collectively forced the climate system past a critical threshold, ending Earth’s longest-lived Phanerozoic icehouse.

item.title: Petrogenesis of ∼1.96 Ga Mafic–Intermediate Intrusions in the Daqingshan Area: Constraints on the Tectonic Evolution of the Khondalite Belt, North China Craton
item.dc:creator: Yundi Gong
Xiaojie Dong
Zhongyuan Xu
Guanghui Lian
Zhenghong Liu
Guoyu Fan
item.description: The Khondalite Belt (KB) is a Paleoproterozoic collisional orogen within the North China Craton (NCC), but its pre-collisional tectonic evolution remains poorly constrained. In this study, we conducted integrated geochronological, in-situ Hf isotopic, and whole-rock geochemical analyses of the mafic–intermediate intrusions exposed in the Daqingshan area in the central segment of the KB to determine their formation ages, petrogenesis, and tectonic setting, thereby further constraining the late Paleoproterozoic tectonic evolution of the KB. Zircon U-Pb dating results show that these rocks crystallized at ∼1.97–1.96 Ga and subsequently underwent metamorphism at ∼1.94–1.92 Ga. These rocks are enriched in light rare earth elements and depleted in high field strength elements, exhibiting geochemical affinities comparable to those of continental margin arc basalts and island arc basalts. In-situ zircon Hf isotopic analyses yield positive εHf(t) values. The available data suggest that the mafic–intermediate intrusions were derived from a hybrid mantle source dominated by asthenospheric mantle and metasomatized by fluids derived from a subducted slab and experienced fractional crystallization of olivine, clinopyroxene, Fe-Ti oxides and apatite. Overall, these findings suggest that the KB was in a subduction-related tectonic setting at ∼1.97–1.96 Ga. Subsequent continent–continent collision at ∼1.95 Ga resulted in metamorphism of both the magmatic rocks and the Khondalite Series at ∼1.94–1.92 Ga. Authors: Yundi Gong; Xiaojie Dong; Zhongyuan Xu; Guanghui Lian; Zhenghong Liu; Guoyu Fan

Journal: Journal of Asian Earth Sciences

DOI: 10.1016/j.jseaes.2026.107215

Relevance: 0.566 (consider)

The Khondalite Belt (KB) is a Paleoproterozoic collisional orogen within the North China Craton (NCC), but its pre-collisional tectonic evolution remains poorly constrained. In this study, we conducted integrated geochronological, in-situ Hf isotopic, and whole-rock geochemical analyses of the mafic–intermediate intrusions exposed in the Daqingshan area in the central segment of the KB to determine their formation ages, petrogenesis, and tectonic setting, thereby further constraining the late Paleoproterozoic tectonic evolution of the KB. Zircon U-Pb dating results show that these rocks crystallized at ∼1.97–1.96 Ga and subsequently underwent metamorphism at ∼1.94–1.92 Ga. These rocks are enriched in light rare earth elements and depleted in high field strength elements, exhibiting geochemical affinities comparable to those of continental margin arc basalts and island arc basalts. In-situ zircon Hf isotopic analyses yield positive εHf(t) values. The available data suggest that the mafic–intermediate intrusions were derived from a hybrid mantle source dominated by asthenospheric mantle and metasomatized by fluids derived from a subducted slab and experienced fractional crystallization of olivine, clinopyroxene, Fe-Ti oxides and apatite. Overall, these findings suggest that the KB was in a subduction-related tectonic setting at ∼1.97–1.96 Ga. Subsequent continent–continent collision at ∼1.95 Ga resulted in metamorphism of both the magmatic rocks and the Khondalite Series at ∼1.94–1.92 Ga.

item.title: Early Jurassic back-arc basin development in the South Qiangtang terrane, Tibetan Plateau: A sedimentary response to the northward subduction of the Meso-Tethys Ocean
item.dc:creator: Yanshen Liu
Zhongjie Xu
Chaoming Xie
Shiyao Gao
Dazhi Xu
item.description: The impact of the northward subduction of the Meso-Tethys Ocean (Bangong-Nujiang Tethyan Ocean) on the tectonic-sedimentary evolution of the South Qiangtang terrane remains poorly constrained. In particular, the lack of systematic studies on sedimentary records within this subduction system has hindered a deeper understanding of the oceanic basin evolution and the oceanic-continental subduction processes. Here, we present an integrated investigation of sedimentology, petrography, zircon Rare Earth Elements geochemistry, and detrital zircon U-Pb geochronology for the Sewa Formation in the Rialuma section along the southern margin of the South Qiangtang terrane and systematically reveal the depositional environment, provenance characteristics, and tectonic setting of the Sewa Formation. Results indicate that the maximum depositional age of the Lower-Middle Jurassic Sewa Formation is constrained to the Early Jurassic (∼177 Ma). Its lower member consists of barrier island-lagoon deposits, while its upper member is dominated by open-shelf deposits. By comparing detrital zircon U-Pb age spectra and performing multi-dimensional scaling analysis with data from Lower Jurassic clastic rocks along the southern margin of Laurasia, we identify the primary sediment sources. These include the South and North Qiangtang terranes, the Longmu Co-Shuanghu Suture Zone (an accretionary complex), and the Amdo microcontinent. Using the Eu anomaly in detrital zircons, we reconstruct the crustal thickness evolution of the South Qiangtang terrane from 260 to 170 Ma. Further, the r _Dz index indicates that the Early Jurassic Sewa Formation was deposited in a continent-continent collision tectonic setting. Synthesizing these new data with previous studies on the Sewa Formation, we propose that the study area was situated in a back-arc basin during the Early Jurassic, with collision and amalgamation occurring between the Amdo microcontinent and South Qiangtang terrane. These findings provide new sedimentary evidence for the Early Jurassic tectonic evolution of the eastern segment of the Meso-Tethys Ocean. Published: 2026-07-20T01:36:02+00:00 Venue: Science China Earth Sciences Score: 0.563 (consider)

The impact of the northward subduction of the Meso-Tethys Ocean (Bangong-Nujiang Tethyan Ocean) on the tectonic-sedimentary evolution of the South Qiangtang terrane remains poorly constrained. In particular, the lack of systematic studies on sedimentary records within this subduction system has hindered a deeper understanding of the oceanic basin evolution and the oceanic-continental subduction processes. Synthesizing these new data with previous studies on the Sewa Formation, we propose that the study area was situated in a back-arc basin during the Early Jurassic, with collision and amalgamation occurring between the Amdo microcontinent and South Qiangtang terrane. These findings provide new sedimentary evidence for the Early Jurassic tectonic evolution of the eastern segment of the Meso-Tethys Ocean.

item.title: Metallogeny of the Mesoproterozoic Belt-Purcell Basin, Northern Rocky Mountains, USA-Canada
item.dc:creator: John F. Slack
item.description: Abstract Metasedimentary rocks of the Mesoproterozoic Belt Supergroup in the United States and correlative Purcell Supergroup in Canada contain diverse types of mineral deposits with a variety of commodities including base metals, precious metals, and critical metals. The economically most important deposits (≥ 10 Mt past production + reserves + resources) are stratabound Pb–Zn–Ag ores at the Sullivan mine in southeastern British Columbia, Ag–Pb–Zn–(Cu) veins of the Coeur d’Alene district in northern Idaho, stratabound sediment-hosted Cu–Ag deposits of the Spar Lake, Montanore, and Rock Creek deposits in western Montana, stratabound Co–Cu–Au–(Bi–Y–REE) ores of the Idaho cobalt belt, and stratiform Cu–Co–Ag at the Black Butte deposit in western Montana. Minor deposits in the region, also hosted in strata of the Belt and Purcell supergroups, are stratabound Pb–Zn–Ag deposits in southeastern British Columbia, and Th–REE–(Cu) veins of the Lemhi Pass district in northern Idaho and western Montana. Small prospects and occurrences include gold-quartz veins, Ag–Pb–Zn veins, Fe–(Cu–Au) deposits, and a variety of rare metals (Th, REE, Nb, Be, Sn) in pegmatites and carbonatites. High-precision geochronology of ore and gangue minerals, like in the Sullivan deposit, has yielded only Mesoproterozoic ages, but other deposits (Blackbird, Rock Creek, Coeur d’Alene, Lemhi Pass) have U–Pb and/or Re–Os ages that suggest Neoproterozoic, Paleozoic, and/or Mesozoic to Tertiary overprinting of older Mesoproterozoic mineralization, possibly linked in some cases to fluid flow during reactivation of deep basement structures. Some of the Paleozoic and younger ages are also attributed here to dissolution-reprecipitation of dated minerals and not to new metal introduction. An integrated assessment of the geochronological data reveals growing evidence for previously underappreciated roles of major mineralizing events occurring during the East Kootenay orogeny (1379 − 1325 Ma) and the Grenvillian orogeny (1150 to 980 Ma), with important implications for mineral exploration in many parts of the Belt-Purcell Basin. Published: 2026-07-17T07:55:50+00:00 Venue: Mineralium Deposita Score: 0.562 (consider)

Authors: John F. Slack

Journal: Mineralium Deposita

DOI: 10.1007/s00126-026-01471-2

Relevance: 0.562 (consider)

Abstract Metasedimentary rocks of the Mesoproterozoic Belt Supergroup in the United States and correlative Purcell Supergroup in Canada contain diverse types of mineral deposits with a variety of commodities including base metals, precious metals, and critical metals. The economically most important deposits (≥ 10 Mt past production + reserves + resources) are stratabound Pb–Zn–Ag ores at the Sullivan mine in southeastern British Columbia, Ag–Pb–Zn–(Cu) veins of the Coeur d’Alene district in northern Idaho, stratabound sediment-hosted Cu–Ag deposits of the Spar Lake, Montanore, and Rock Creek deposits in western Montana, stratabound Co–Cu–Au–(Bi–Y–REE) ores of the Idaho cobalt belt, and stratiform Cu–Co–Ag at the Black Butte deposit in western Montana. Minor deposits in the region, also hosted in strata of the Belt and Purcell supergroups, are stratabound Pb–Zn–Ag deposits in southeastern British Columbia, and Th–REE–(Cu) veins of the Lemhi Pass district in northern Idaho and western Montana. Small prospects and occurrences include gold-quartz veins, Ag–Pb–Zn veins, Fe–(Cu–Au) deposits, and a variety of rare metals (Th, REE, Nb, Be, Sn) in pegmatites and carbonatites. High-precision geochronology of ore and gangue minerals, like in the Sullivan deposit, has yielded only Mesoproterozoic ages, but other deposits (Blackbird, Rock Creek, Coeur d’Alene, Lemhi Pass) have U–Pb and/or Re–Os ages that suggest Neoproterozoic, Paleozoic, and/or Mesozoic to Tertiary overprinting of older Mesoproterozoic mineralization, possibly linked in some cases to fluid flow during reactivation of deep basement structures. Some of the Paleozoic and younger ages are also attributed here to dissolution-reprecipitation of dated minerals and not to new metal introduction. An integrated assessment of the geochronological data reveals growing evidence for previously underappreciated roles of major mineralizing events occurring during the East Kootenay orogeny (1379 − 1325 Ma) and the Grenvillian orogeny (1150 to 980 Ma), with important implications for mineral exploration in many parts of the Belt-Purcell Basin.

item.title: In-situ elemental compositions and U-Pb ages of garnet and vesuvianite from the Cu-Zn skarn in the Dachang Sn-polymetallic district, South China
item.dc:creator: Jiadai Li
Jing Xu
Liyuan Wang
Taiping Zhao
item.description: The Youjiang tin belt represents a globally significant Sn-polymetallic belt located at the junction between the Tethyan and Paleo-Pacific tectonic domains, yet its tectonic setting and relationship to adjacent tin belts remain poorly understood. Precise geochronological constraints are essential for deciphering ore genesis and its geodynamic evolution. This study presents U-Pb dating of garnet and vesuvianite for the Tongkeng-Yangjiaojian, Lamo and Chashan deposits within the world-class Dachang district, providing robust temporal constraints on its metallogenesis. Two garnet samples from the Tongkeng-Yangjiaojian skarns yield lower intercept ages of 92.8 ± 4.2 Ma and 92.1 ± 3.6 Ma, which are indistinguishable within analytical uncertainty from those obtained for the Lamo (93.9 ± 1.1 Ma) and Chashan (93 ± 10 Ma) deposits. In addition, vesuvianite from the Tongkeng skarn yields a U-Pb age of 90.6 ± 1.1 Ma. Trace element systematics reveal that U concentrations in all analyzed garnet samples correlate positively with LREEs rather than HREEs, indicating that U is predominantly incorporated into garnet as U4+ and occupies the X site through a mechanism similar to that governing LREEs partitioning. Positive correlations between U and W, Sn and In further suggest that a proportion of U also enters the Y site as U6+. The geochemical compositions of garnet and vesuvianite indicate that the Dachang skarn formed from the relatively oxidized, high-salinity magmatic-hydrothermal fluids. Integrated with previously published dates for the granite-related tin deposits across the adjacent Nanling Range, Southeastern Coast and Southeast Asian tin belts, these new ages support a model in which Late Cretaceous tin mineralization in the Youjiang tin belt formed in a back-arc extensional setting associated with rollback of the Neo-Tethyan oceanic slab. Published: 2026-07-16T15:35:38+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.560 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jiadai Li; Jing Xu; Liyuan Wang; Taiping Zhao

Journal: Lithos

DOI: 10.1016/j.lithos.2026.108714

Relevance: 0.560 (consider)

The Youjiang tin belt represents a globally significant Sn-polymetallic belt located at the junction between the Tethyan and Paleo-Pacific tectonic domains, yet its tectonic setting and relationship to adjacent tin belts remain poorly understood. Precise geochronological constraints are essential for deciphering ore genesis and its geodynamic evolution. This study presents U-Pb dating of garnet and vesuvianite for the Tongkeng-Yangjiaojian, Lamo and Chashan deposits within the world-class Dachang district, providing robust temporal constraints on its metallogenesis. Two garnet samples from the Tongkeng-Yangjiaojian skarns yield lower intercept ages of 92.8 ± 4.2 Ma and 92.1 ± 3.6 Ma, which are indistinguishable within analytical uncertainty from those obtained for the Lamo (93.9 ± 1.1 Ma) and Chashan (93 ± 10 Ma) deposits. In addition, vesuvianite from the Tongkeng skarn yields a U-Pb age of 90.6 ± 1.1 Ma. Trace element systematics reveal that U concentrations in all analyzed garnet samples correlate positively with LREEs rather than HREEs, indicating that U is predominantly incorporated into garnet as U4+ and occupies the X site through a mechanism similar to that governing LREEs partitioning. Positive correlations between U and W, Sn and In further suggest that a proportion of U also enters the Y site as U6+. The geochemical compositions of garnet and vesuvianite indicate that the Dachang skarn formed from the relatively oxidized, high-salinity magmatic-hydrothermal fluids. Integrated with previously published dates for the granite-related tin deposits across the adjacent Nanling Range, Southeastern Coast and Southeast Asian tin belts, these new ages support a model in which Late Cretaceous tin mineralization in the Youjiang tin belt formed in a back-arc extensional setting associated with rollback of the Neo-Tethyan oceanic slab.

item.title: Indium enrichment in sphalerite from magmatic-hydrothermal systems: Insights from the Sandaoqiao Pb-Zn-Ag deposit, the North Greater Khingan Range, NE China
item.dc:creator: Junchen Liu
Yitian Wang
Qiaoqing Hu
Jingwen Mao
Dazhao Wang
Meng He
Qiangbing Guan
Zhihui Duan
Shun'’an Dang
item.description: The Sandaoqiao Pb-Zn-Ag deposit, located within the Erguna metallogenic belt of the Greater Khingan Range in NE China, contains exceptionally high indium (In) concentrations (up to 6000 ppm in sphalerite). This study provides a comprehensive analysis of In mineralization in the deposit, combining field geology, petrography, precise geochronology, in-situ sulfur isotope data, and trace element microanalysis. Zircon U-Pb ages from pre- and post-ore dikes bracket the mineralization event between 136.0 ± 0.7 Ma and 120.8 ± 0.6 Ma, while U-Pb dating of hydrothermal rutile yields a direct ore-forming age of 120.8 ± 5.8 Ma, contemporaneous with Early Cretaceous granite emplacement. In-situ δ34S values of sulfides range from 2.3‰ to 5.6‰, supporting a magmatic-hydrothermal origin. Sphalerite is the primary host mineral for In, with two generations (Sp-I and Sp-II) identified that record a progressive enrichment trend of In. The early Fe-rich Sp-I contains abundant chalcopyrite inclusions and has In concentrations of 70.3–443.3 ppm, whereas the late Sp-II without inclusions, exhibits significantly higher In contents (107.1–6037.1 ppm). Trace element mapping and correlation analysis indicate that In incorporation occurs mainly through the coupled substitution mechanism In3+ + Cu+ ↔ 2Zn2+. LA-ICP-MS data and physicochemical modeling suggest that the residence state of Cu in sphalerite, coupled with low temperature, reduced, and acidic hydrothermal conditions, facilitated pronounced In enrichment in Sp-II. These results offer new insights on the behavior of In during sphalerite crystallization in magmatic-hydrothermal systems. It further highlights the Sandaoqiao Pb-Zn(-Ag) deposit as a high potential In resource and identifies the Erguna metallogenic belt as a prospective target for In exploration. Published: 2026-07-17T16:00:12+00:00 Venue: Journal of Geochemical Exploration Score: 0.560 (consider)

The Sandaoqiao Pb-Zn-Ag deposit, located within the Erguna metallogenic belt of the Greater Khingan Range in NE China, contains exceptionally high indium (In) concentrations (up to 6000 ppm in sphalerite). This study provides a comprehensive analysis of In mineralization in the deposit, combining field geology, petrography, precise geochronology, in-situ sulfur isotope data, and trace element microanalysis. Zircon U-Pb ages from pre- and post-ore dikes bracket the mineralization event between 136.0 ± 0.7 Ma and 120.8 ± 0.6 Ma, while U-Pb dating of hydrothermal rutile yields a direct ore-forming age of 120.8 ± 5.8 Ma, contemporaneous with Early Cretaceous granite emplacement. In-situ δ34S values of sulfides range from 2.3‰ to 5.6‰, supporting a magmatic-hydrothermal origin. Sphalerite is the primary host mineral for In, with two generations (Sp-I and Sp-II) identified that record a progressive enrichment trend of In. The early Fe-rich Sp-I contains abundant chalcopyrite inclusions and has In concentrations of 70.3–443.3 ppm, whereas the late Sp-II without inclusions, exhibits significantly higher In contents (107.1–6037.1 ppm). Trace element mapping and correlation analysis indicate that In incorporation occurs mainly through the coupled substitution mechanism In3+ + Cu+ ↔ 2Zn2+. LA-ICP-MS data and physicochemical modeling suggest that the residence state of Cu in sphalerite, coupled with low temperature, reduced, and acidic hydrothermal conditions, facilitated pronounced In enrichment in Sp-II. These results offer new insights on the behavior of In during sphalerite crystallization in magmatic-hydrothermal systems. It further highlights the Sandaoqiao Pb-Zn(-Ag) deposit as a high potential In resource and identifies the Erguna metallogenic belt as a prospective target for In exploration.

item.title: Oxidation of Aqueous Sulfide by Chlorate: Implications for Native Sulfur Formation on Mars
item.dc:creator: J. Wang
S. Kim
Z. Gold
R. L. Cheng
C. Muñoz‐Saez
N. M. Fernandez
item.description: Abstract The recent discovery of native sulfur (“sulfur stones” consist of elemental sulfur) by Curiosity rover in the Gale Crater has sparked considerable research interest toward identifying its formation mechanisms that could generate novel insights into Martian sulfur cycling. This study examines aqueous, abiotic oxidation of sulfide by chlorate (ClO 3 − ) as a plausible pathway for native sulfur production on Early Mars. A series of controlled anaerobic batch experiments were performed under acidic conditions (pH ∼ 1.7) and ambient temperature (22°C) across a range of dissolved chlorate concentrations (0–50 mM) for a 54‐day duration. Spectroscopic analyses (XRD, Raman, SEM‐EDS) of precipitates formed from experiments and observed changes in fluid chemistry confirmed the formation of elemental sulfur and sulfate. Our findings provide the first successful demonstration of elemental sulfur formation via abiotic chlorate‐driven homogeneous oxidation of aqueous sulfide and highlight the potential for coupled S‐Cl cycling as important drivers of redox transformation on Mars. Published: 2026-07-21T08:43:51+00:00 Venue: Geophysical Research Letters Score: 0.558 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: J. Wang; S. Kim; Z. Gold; R. L. Cheng; C. Muñoz‐Saez; N. M. Fernandez

Journal: Geophysical Research Letters

DOI: 10.1029/2025gl121494

Relevance: 0.558 (consider)

Abstract The recent discovery of native sulfur (“sulfur stones” consist of elemental sulfur) by Curiosity rover in the Gale Crater has sparked considerable research interest toward identifying its formation mechanisms that could generate novel insights into Martian sulfur cycling. This study examines aqueous, abiotic oxidation of sulfide by chlorate (ClO 3 − ) as a plausible pathway for native sulfur production on Early Mars. A series of controlled anaerobic batch experiments were performed under acidic conditions (pH ∼ 1.7) and ambient temperature (22°C) across a range of dissolved chlorate concentrations (0–50 mM) for a 54‐day duration. Spectroscopic analyses (XRD, Raman, SEM‐EDS) of precipitates formed from experiments and observed changes in fluid chemistry confirmed the formation of elemental sulfur and sulfate. Our findings provide the first successful demonstration of elemental sulfur formation via abiotic chlorate‐driven homogeneous oxidation of aqueous sulfide and highlight the potential for coupled S‐Cl cycling as important drivers of redox transformation on Mars.

item.title: Super-lithostatic fluid overpressure–tectonic coupling in horizontal opening-mode fracturing and blocky cementation in the crust
item.dc:creator: Miao Wang
XinXin Gao
Yelei Wang
Yong Chen
Daniel Moncada
Fang Hao
Tao Hu
Fulai Li
Yifan Song
Ziye Lu
Xin Zeng
Zhiming Zhang
Enze Wang
Matthew Steele-MacInnis
item.description: Horizontal hydraulic opening-mode fractures are widely developed across sedimentary, magmatic, and metamorphic crustal environments. For the general case where the vertical stress corresponds to the lithostatic load, such fracturing is favored under compressive stress regimes at low differential stress, when the fluid pressures exceeding the lithostatic load (i.e., achieving super-lithostatic fluid overpressures). In many cases these fractures contain blocky mineral fills, indicating that deposition occurred within an open, fluid-filled space owing to a lack of spanning during opening. Some evidence from primary fluid inclusions within these blocky cements, along with some conceptual interpretation from all three crustal systems, demonstrate that super-lithostatic fluid overpressures could also be present during syn-kinematic blocky cementations within these fractures, which provided the potential for maintaining them open against the lithostatic load and accommodating blocky crystallization. However, the mechanical significance of such fluid-pressure–tectonic coupling, particularly the role of super-lithostatic fluid overpressure in sustaining horizontal fractures open and blocky cementation have not yet been systematically synthesized across crustal environments. In this review, we focus on the scenarios where super-lithostatic fluid overpressure does occur, and its coupling with tectonic stresses to facilitate horizontal hydraulic opening-mode fracturing and blocky cementation in the crust. Some case studies across diverse systems demonstrate that super-lithostatic fluid overpressures—generated via gas generation during thermal cracking in sedimentary shales, volatile exsolution within intrusion roof zones in magmatic systems (e.g., porphyry-epithermal ore deposit, granite-related Sn-W deposit and pegmatite), or devolatilization reactions in metamorphic orogenic gold belts—consistently coupled with compressive regimes to drive horizontal hydraulic fracturing and sustain open spaces for blocky deposition. Taken together, this synthesis highlights that super-lithostatic fluid overpressure plays a pivotal dual role in both forming horizontal opening-mode fracturing and mechanically maintaining the open spaces required for subsequent blocky cementation, a mechanism that directly controls crustal fluid focusing and mineral accumulation, particularly for sedimentary hydrocarbons, orogenic precious metals, and magmatic critical minerals. Published: 2026-07-20T06:55:47+00:00 Venue: Earth-Science Reviews Score: 0.555 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Miao Wang; XinXin Gao; Yelei Wang; Yong Chen; Daniel Moncada; Fang Hao; Tao Hu; Fulai Li; Yifan Song; Ziye Lu; Xin Zeng; Zhiming Zhang; Enze Wang; Matthew Steele-MacInnis

Journal: Earth-Science Reviews

DOI: 10.1016/j.earscirev.2026.105630

Relevance: 0.555 (consider)

Horizontal hydraulic opening-mode fractures are widely developed across sedimentary, magmatic, and metamorphic crustal environments. For the general case where the vertical stress corresponds to the lithostatic load, such fracturing is favored under compressive stress regimes at low differential stress, when the fluid pressures exceeding the lithostatic load (i.e., achieving super-lithostatic fluid overpressures). In many cases these fractures contain blocky mineral fills, indicating that deposition occurred within an open, fluid-filled space owing to a lack of spanning during opening. Some evidence from primary fluid inclusions within these blocky cements, along with some conceptual interpretation from all three crustal systems, demonstrate that super-lithostatic fluid overpressures could also be present during syn-kinematic blocky cementations within these fractures, which provided the potential for maintaining them open against the lithostatic load and accommodating blocky crystallization. However, the mechanical significance of such fluid-pressure–tectonic coupling, particularly the role of super-lithostatic fluid overpressure in sustaining horizontal fractures open and blocky cementation have not yet been systematically synthesized across crustal environments. In this review, we focus on the scenarios where super-lithostatic fluid overpressure does occur, and its coupling with tectonic stresses to facilitate horizontal hydraulic opening-mode fracturing and blocky cementation in the crust. Some case studies across diverse systems demonstrate that super-lithostatic fluid overpressures—generated via gas generation during thermal cracking in sedimentary shales, volatile exsolution within intrusion roof zones in magmatic systems (e.g., porphyry-epithermal ore deposit, granite-related Sn-W deposit and pegmatite), or devolatilization reactions in metamorphic orogenic gold belts—consistently coupled with compressive regimes to drive horizontal hydraulic fracturing and sustain open spaces for blocky deposition. Taken together, this synthesis highlights that super-lithostatic fluid overpressure plays a pivotal dual role in both forming horizontal opening-mode fracturing and mechanically maintaining the open spaces required for subsequent blocky cementation, a mechanism that directly controls crustal fluid focusing and mineral accumulation, particularly for sedimentary hydrocarbons, orogenic precious metals, and magmatic critical minerals.

item.title: Geochronological and geochemical insights into Neoproterozoic granitoids of the Guibei region, western Jiangnan Orogen, China: Implications for petrogenesis and tectonic evolution
item.dc:creator: Youyue Lu
Jingya Cao
Liyan Ma
Jianming Fu
Alexander L. Peace
Xiaoyong Yang
Jiaxi Zhou
Shunbo Cheng
Kun Li
Bang An
item.description: The Jiangnan Orogen (JO) is widely acknowledged as the suture zone between the Yangtze and Cathaysia Blocks in South China, yet the precise timing of their collision remains a subject of ongoing debate. This study investigates the Neoproterozoic granitoids (granites and granodiorites) in the Guibei region of the western JO, utilizing geochronological, geochemical, and isotopic analyses to constrain their petrogenesis and the tectonic evolution of the JO. Zircon U–Pb dating results show that both the Neoproterozoic granites and granodiorites in the study area formed during the interval of 830–833 Ma. Geochemically, the granites exhibit typical S-type characteristics, including high A/CNK ratios (1.08–1.42) and low Ga/Al ratios (2.18–2.88). Their εNd(t) values range from –6.24 to –5.09, with predominantly negative εHf(t) values, indicating that they were derived from the partial melting of crustal basement rocks, followed by subsequent fractional crystallization. In contrast, the granodiorites belong to the peraluminous I-type granite category with high A/CNK ratios (1.20–1.46) and CaO (1.48–3.31%) contents, and low Ga/Al ratios (1.99–2.12). These granodiorites also display decoupled Nd-Hf isotopes, with εNd(t) values of –5.68 to –5.50 and εHf(t) values of –4.1 to 11.6, indicating their petrogenesis is attributed to the mixing of mantle- and crustal-derived magmas, accompanied by the residual presence of garnet. Combined with the previous data, this evidence implies that the collision between the Yangtze and Cathaysia Blocks might have occurred at approximately 850–830 Ma, providing critical constraints on the Precambrian tectonic evolution of South China. Published: 2026-07-18T15:29:12+00:00 Venue: Journal of Asian Earth Sciences Score: 0.555 (consider)

Authors: Youyue Lu; Jingya Cao; Liyan Ma; Jianming Fu; Alexander L. Peace; Xiaoyong Yang; Jiaxi Zhou; Shunbo Cheng; Kun Li; Bang An

Journal: Journal of Asian Earth Sciences

DOI: 10.1016/j.jseaes.2026.107228

Relevance: 0.555 (consider)

The Jiangnan Orogen (JO) is widely acknowledged as the suture zone between the Yangtze and Cathaysia Blocks in South China, yet the precise timing of their collision remains a subject of ongoing debate. This study investigates the Neoproterozoic granitoids (granites and granodiorites) in the Guibei region of the western JO, utilizing geochronological, geochemical, and isotopic analyses to constrain their petrogenesis and the tectonic evolution of the JO. Zircon U–Pb dating results show that both the Neoproterozoic granites and granodiorites in the study area formed during the interval of 830–833 Ma. Geochemically, the granites exhibit typical S-type characteristics, including high A/CNK ratios (1.08–1.42) and low Ga/Al ratios (2.18–2.88). Their εNd(t) values range from –6.24 to –5.09, with predominantly negative εHf(t) values, indicating that they were derived from the partial melting of crustal basement rocks, followed by subsequent fractional crystallization. In contrast, the granodiorites belong to the peraluminous I-type granite category with high A/CNK ratios (1.20–1.46) and CaO (1.48–3.31%) contents, and low Ga/Al ratios (1.99–2.12). These granodiorites also display decoupled Nd-Hf isotopes, with εNd(t) values of –5.68 to –5.50 and εHf(t) values of –4.1 to 11.6, indicating their petrogenesis is attributed to the mixing of mantle- and crustal-derived magmas, accompanied by the residual presence of garnet. Combined with the previous data, this evidence implies that the collision between the Yangtze and Cathaysia Blocks might have occurred at approximately 850–830 Ma, providing critical constraints on the Precambrian tectonic evolution of South China.

item.title: Geochemical, Fluid Inclusions and Stable Isotopes Constraint on the Genesis of the Hendouabad Cu–Au Mineralization, Central Urumieh–Dokhtar Magmatic Arc, Iran
item.dc:creator: Maryam Tarmas Hafshejani
Soroush Modabberi
Davood Raeisi
Dunya Maronsi
item.description: ABSTRACT The Hendouabad Cu–Au deposit, located in the central Urumieh–Dokhtar Magmatic Arc, represents a newly explored example of an epithermal deposit hosted by Late Eocene volcanic rocks of basaltic andesite to andesite composition. Mineralization occurs in two zones: A Cu–Au zone in the east and a Cu‐only zone in the west hosted by andesite, basalt and associated dikes. Ore minerals include pyrite, chalcopyrite, chalcocite, galena and bornite, and enargite with supergene assemblages of malachite, haematite and chrysocolla. Hydrothermal alteration is dominated by propylitic and silicic assemblages. Fluid inclusions yielded homogenization temperatures of 109–280°C and low‐to‐moderate salinities (1.6–9.0 wt% NaCl equiv.), with petrographic evidence of boiling. Stable isotope analyses indicate mixed magmatic–meteoric signatures, with quartz‐ and epidote‐derived δ 18 O fluid values ranging from +2.2‰ to +14.2‰ and sulphide δ 34 S values from (−12.0‰ to −5.3‰, CDT). These results suggest that ore‐forming fluids were derived from a magmatic source but were strongly modified by meteoric water infiltration and evolving redox conditions. The combined geological, petrographic, geochemical, fluid inclusion and isotopic evidence supports classification of the Hendouabad deposit as a structurally controlled low‐to‐intermediate sulphidation epithermal Cu–Au system formed through boiling, mixing and cooling of magmatic–meteoric hydrothermal fluids. Published: 2026-07-17T13:50:35+00:00 Venue: Acta Geologica Sinica - English Edition Score: 0.553 (consider)

Journal: Acta Geologica Sinica - English Edition

ABSTRACT The Hendouabad Cu–Au deposit, located in the central Urumieh–Dokhtar Magmatic Arc, represents a newly explored example of an epithermal deposit hosted by Late Eocene volcanic rocks of basaltic andesite to andesite composition. Mineralization occurs in two zones: A Cu–Au zone in the east and a Cu‐only zone in the west hosted by andesite, basalt and associated dikes. Ore minerals include pyrite, chalcopyrite, chalcocite, galena and bornite, and enargite with supergene assemblages of malachite, haematite and chrysocolla. Hydrothermal alteration is dominated by propylitic and silicic assemblages. Fluid inclusions yielded homogenization temperatures of 109–280°C and low‐to‐moderate salinities (1.6–9.0 wt% NaCl equiv.), with petrographic evidence of boiling. Stable isotope analyses indicate mixed magmatic–meteoric signatures, with quartz‐ and epidote‐derived δ 18 O fluid values ranging from +2.2‰ to +14.2‰ and sulphide δ 34 S values from (−12.0‰ to −5.3‰, CDT). These results suggest that ore‐forming fluids were derived from a magmatic source but were strongly modified by meteoric water infiltration and evolving redox conditions. The combined geological, petrographic, geochemical, fluid inclusion and isotopic evidence supports classification of the Hendouabad deposit as a structurally controlled low‐to‐intermediate sulphidation epithermal Cu–Au system formed through boiling, mixing and cooling of magmatic–meteoric hydrothermal fluids.

item.title: Complexly zoned clinopyroxenes and amphiboles from the end-Cretaceous alkaline lamprophyres of the Nir-Wandh igneous complex, NW Deccan Traps, India: Insights into trans-lithospheric magmatic plumbing system processes
item.dc:creator: Abhinay Sharma
Rohit Pandey
N.V. Chalapathi Rao
A.D. Shukla
Samarendra Sahoo
Prashant Dhote
item.description: The Nir-Wandh Igneous Complex (67 Ma) of the Kutch region, NW India, represents one of the earliest manifestations of alkaline magmatism within the Deccan Large Igneous Province. This study investigates the petrology and geochemistry of four camptonitic alkaline lamprophyre dykes which are characterized by complexly zoned clinopyroxenes and amphiboles, with minor apatite, FeTi oxides, and feldspar confined to the groundmass. Based on the presence or absence of olivine and the nature of clinopyroxene populations, the four lamprophyre dykes are divided into three groups: Group-A, Group-B, and Group-C. Four clinopyroxene varieties are identified: (i) P1-zoned colourless core phenocrysts with Cr-Mg-rich diopside cores and Ti-rich augite rims; (ii) P2-unzoned colourless Fe-diopside phenocrysts; (iii) P3-green core antecrysts zoned from Na-rich salite to diopside; and (iv) P4-grey core antecrysts zoned from salite to diopside. P1 occurs in Groups A and B, both P3 and P4 are restricted to the Group A, and Group C contains only P2. Three amphibole parageneses are also recognised, ranging from Ti-rich ferro-pargasite and kaersutite to Ti-rich magnesio-hastingsite, each with distinct host-rock associations. Bulk-rock geochemistry and isotopic compositions 87Sr/86Sri (0.70407 to 0.70441) and 143Nd/144Ndi (0.51279 to 0.51286) are similar across the lamprophyres and associated rocks, indicating a common origin. Trace-element modelling of melts in equilibrium with clinopyroxene suggests involvement of two distinct pulses of co-genetic magmas: (i) a camptonitic magma and (ii) a Na-Nb-Zr enriched evolved magma. Amphibole zoning and disequilibrium textures record inherited Fe-rich antecrystic cores and later Mg-rich phenocrystic rims, reflecting interaction between evolved and replenishing magmas. Textural evidence, mineral chemistry, and P-T estimates indicate prolonged fractionation, magma recharge and mixing, crystal recycling, and accumulation within multiple trans-lithospheric reservoirs. Thermobarometry suggests that the Kutch lithosphere was at least ~70 km thick during emplacement. These results demonstrate that pyroxene and amphibole zoning in lamprophyres provides valuable constraints on magma generation depths, mixing processes, and the architecture of trans-lithospheric magmatic plumbing systems. Published: 2026-07-16T05:20:42+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.552 (consider)

Relevance: 0.552 (consider)

The Nir-Wandh Igneous Complex (67 Ma) of the Kutch region, NW India, represents one of the earliest manifestations of alkaline magmatism within the Deccan Large Igneous Province. This study investigates the petrology and geochemistry of four camptonitic alkaline lamprophyre dykes which are characterized by complexly zoned clinopyroxenes and amphiboles, with minor apatite, FeTi oxides, and feldspar confined to the groundmass. Four clinopyroxene varieties are identified: (i) P1-zoned colourless core phenocrysts with Cr-Mg-rich diopside cores and Ti-rich augite rims; (ii) P2-unzoned colourless Fe-diopside phenocrysts; (iii) P3-green core antecrysts zoned from Na-rich salite to diopside; and (iv) P4-grey core antecrysts zoned from salite to diopside. P1 occurs in Groups A and B, both P3 and P4 are restricted to the Group A, and Group C contains only P2. Three amphibole parageneses are also recognised, ranging from Ti-rich ferro-pargasite and kaersutite to Ti-rich magnesio-hastingsite, each with distinct host-rock associations. Bulk-rock geochemistry and isotopic compositions 87Sr/86Sri (0.70407 to 0.70441) and 143Nd/144Ndi (0.51279 to 0.51286) are similar across the lamprophyres and associated rocks, indicating a common origin. Trace-element modelling of melts in equilibrium with clinopyroxene suggests involvement of two distinct pulses of co-genetic magmas: (i) a camptonitic magma and (ii) a Na-Nb-Zr enriched evolved magma. Amphibole zoning and disequilibrium textures record inherited Fe-rich antecrystic cores and later Mg-rich phenocrystic rims, reflecting interaction between evolved and replenishing magmas. These results demonstrate that pyroxene and amphibole zoning in lamprophyres provides valuable constraints on magma generation depths, mixing processes, and the architecture of trans-lithospheric magmatic plumbing systems.

item.title: Coupled continental and reverse weathering influence on Cenozoic cooling revealed by the marine lithium cycle
item.dc:creator: Pan Zhang
Kang-Jun Huang
item.description: The Cenozoic transition from greenhouse to icehouse climate has been commonly attributed to enhanced silicate weathering driven by tectonic uplift and intensified erosion. However, the manner in which increased physical erosion translates into climate cooling via regulation of the long-term carbon-silicon cycle remains poorly constrained. We apply coupled seawater lithium concentration and lithium isotope composition to reconstruct the Cenozoic evolution of the marine Li cycle and associated surficial C-Si processes. Our results show a pronounced increase in riverine δ7Li (∼13‰) over the Cenozoic accompanied by a modest rise (0.5×) in weathering flux, but a modest decline (0.6×) in marine authigenic clay formation (reverse weathering). These trends indicate that with progressive denudation and climate cooling, weathering congruency significantly declined, reducing CO2 consumption and thereby allowing silicate weathering fluxes to rise only slightly. Concurrently, suppressed reverse weathering reflects a reorganization of marine Si cycling, which further reduced CO2 release to the atmosphere. Our refined marine Li cycle suggests that both continental silicate weathering and reverse weathering contributed to Cenozoic cooling, likely reflecting a reorganization of the global C-Si cycle. Published: 2026-07-17T02:05:53+00:00 Venue: Geology Score: 0.551 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Pan Zhang; Kang-Jun Huang

Journal: Geology

DOI: 10.1130/g54403.1

Relevance: 0.551 (consider)

The Cenozoic transition from greenhouse to icehouse climate has been commonly attributed to enhanced silicate weathering driven by tectonic uplift and intensified erosion. However, the manner in which increased physical erosion translates into climate cooling via regulation of the long-term carbon-silicon cycle remains poorly constrained. We apply coupled seawater lithium concentration and lithium isotope composition to reconstruct the Cenozoic evolution of the marine Li cycle and associated surficial C-Si processes. Our results show a pronounced increase in riverine δ7Li (∼13‰) over the Cenozoic accompanied by a modest rise (0.5×) in weathering flux, but a modest decline (0.6×) in marine authigenic clay formation (reverse weathering). These trends indicate that with progressive denudation and climate cooling, weathering congruency significantly declined, reducing CO2 consumption and thereby allowing silicate weathering fluxes to rise only slightly. Concurrently, suppressed reverse weathering reflects a reorganization of marine Si cycling, which further reduced CO2 release to the atmosphere. Our refined marine Li cycle suggests that both continental silicate weathering and reverse weathering contributed to Cenozoic cooling, likely reflecting a reorganization of the global C-Si cycle.

item.title: Geochronology and geochemistry of titanite from the Haita uranium deposit: Constraints on genesis and evolution of coarse-grained uraninite
item.dc:creator: Kai Zhao
Fang An
Yan Zhang
Guangrong Li
Kaiyun Chen
Zhian Bao
Bailin Wu
Yipeng Zhou
Zhanxue Sun
Honglin Yuan
item.description: Coarse-grained uraninite in the Haita area (Miyi County, Sichuan Province, China) is characterized by exceptionally large crystal sizes and euhedral forms. However, uraninite in this district has experienced substantial post-ore alteration. This study therefore focuses on well-preserved remnant titanite domains for in-situ analysis to minimize screening costs and ensure data reliability. Titanite U–Pb analysis yields a concordant age of 765.5 ± 8.6 Ma, representing the crystallization age of uraninite and indicating a Neoproterozoic mineralization event. Titanite geochemical analysis indicates that the ore-forming fluids were enriched in F–H2O and other mineralizing agents, with REE patterns comparable to those of coarse-grained uraninite and high-temperature magmatic uraninite deposits. Thermobarometric estimates reveal that titanite crystallized at 650–770 °C and 160–260 MPa. The uraninite–titanite–apatite–albite mineral assemblage and Ce–Eu anomalies collectively indicate that the ore-forming fluids were Ti4+-P5+-F−-Na+-rich and reducing. Uranium migrated as U4+–F− complexes in the fluids and precipitated as coarse-grained uraninite upon slow cooling and complex decomposition. Following crystallization, the U–Pb system remained open, yielding a discordant age cluster at 400–200 Ma that may record a thermal event, though further verification is required. In conclusion, remnant titanite can constrain the formation processes and crystallization age of coarse-grained uraninite. Published: 2026-07-16T15:03:13+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.550 (ignore)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Kai Zhao; Fang An; Yan Zhang; Guangrong Li; Kaiyun Chen; Zhian Bao; Bailin Wu; Yipeng Zhou; Zhanxue Sun; Honglin Yuan

Journal: Ore Geology Reviews

DOI: 10.1016/j.oregeorev.2026.107443

Relevance: 0.550 (ignore)

Coarse-grained uraninite in the Haita area (Miyi County, Sichuan Province, China) is characterized by exceptionally large crystal sizes and euhedral forms. However, uraninite in this district has experienced substantial post-ore alteration. This study therefore focuses on well-preserved remnant titanite domains for in-situ analysis to minimize screening costs and ensure data reliability. Titanite U–Pb analysis yields a concordant age of 765.5 ± 8.6 Ma, representing the crystallization age of uraninite and indicating a Neoproterozoic mineralization event. Titanite geochemical analysis indicates that the ore-forming fluids were enriched in F–H2O and other mineralizing agents, with REE patterns comparable to those of coarse-grained uraninite and high-temperature magmatic uraninite deposits. Thermobarometric estimates reveal that titanite crystallized at 650–770 °C and 160–260 MPa. The uraninite–titanite–apatite–albite mineral assemblage and Ce–Eu anomalies collectively indicate that the ore-forming fluids were Ti4+-P5+-F−-Na+-rich and reducing. Uranium migrated as U4+–F− complexes in the fluids and precipitated as coarse-grained uraninite upon slow cooling and complex decomposition. Following crystallization, the U–Pb system remained open, yielding a discordant age cluster at 400–200 Ma that may record a thermal event, though further verification is required. In conclusion, remnant titanite can constrain the formation processes and crystallization age of coarse-grained uraninite.

item.title: Beyond Equilibrium: Kinetic Thresholds and Rheological Feedbacks Create a Potentially Complex 410 in Slab Regions
item.dc:creator: Buchanan Kerswell
John Wheeler
Rene Gassmöller
J. Huw Davies
Isabel Papanagnou
Sanne Cottaar
item.prism:publicationName: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth
item.description: Abstract The seismic expression of Earth's 410 km discontinuity varies across tectonic settings, from sharp, high‐amplitude interfaces to broad transitions—patterns that cannot be explained by equilibrium thermodynamics without invoking large‐scale thermal or compositional heterogeneities. Laboratory experiments show the olivine wadsleyite phase transition responsible for the 410 is rate‐limited, yet previous numerical studies have not directly evaluated the sensitivity of 410 structure to kinetic and rheological factors. Here we investigate these relationships by coupling a grain‐scale, interface‐controlled olivine wadsleyite growth model to compressible simulations of mantle plumes and subducting slabs. We vary kinetic parameters across seven orders of magnitude and quantify the resulting 410 displacements and widths. Our results reveal an asymmetry between hot and cold environments. In plumes, high temperatures produce sharp 410s (2–3 km wide) regardless of kinetics. In slabs, kinetics exert first‐order control on 410 structure through three regimes: (a) quasi‐equilibrium conditions producing narrow, uplifted 410s and continuous slab descent; (b) intermediate reaction rates generating broader, deeper 410s with metastable olivine wedges resisting downward slab motion; and (c) ultra‐sluggish reaction rates causing slab stagnation with re‐sharpened, deeply displaced 410s ( 100 km). Rheological contrasts modulate these kinetic effects by controlling slab geometry and residence time in the phase transition zone. These findings demonstrate that reaction rates strongly influence 410 structure in subduction zones, establishing the 410 as a potential seismological constraint on upper mantle kinetic processes, particularly in cold environments where disequilibrium effects are amplified. Published: 2026-07-17T19:11:05+00:00 Venue: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth Score: 0.549 (ignore)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Buchanan Kerswell; John Wheeler; Rene Gassmöller; J. Huw Davies; Isabel Papanagnou; Sanne Cottaar

Journal: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth

DOI: 10.1029/2026jb033781

Relevance: 0.549 (ignore)

Abstract The seismic expression of Earth's 410 km discontinuity varies across tectonic settings, from sharp, high‐amplitude interfaces to broad transitions—patterns that cannot be explained by equilibrium thermodynamics without invoking large‐scale thermal or compositional heterogeneities. Laboratory experiments show the olivine wadsleyite phase transition responsible for the 410 is rate‐limited, yet previous numerical studies have not directly evaluated the sensitivity of 410 structure to kinetic and rheological factors. Here we investigate these relationships by coupling a grain‐scale, interface‐controlled olivine wadsleyite growth model to compressible simulations of mantle plumes and subducting slabs. We vary kinetic parameters across seven orders of magnitude and quantify the resulting 410 displacements and widths. Our results reveal an asymmetry between hot and cold environments. In plumes, high temperatures produce sharp 410s (2–3 km wide) regardless of kinetics. In slabs, kinetics exert first‐order control on 410 structure through three regimes: (a) quasi‐equilibrium conditions producing narrow, uplifted 410s and continuous slab descent; (b) intermediate reaction rates generating broader, deeper 410s with metastable olivine wedges resisting downward slab motion; and (c) ultra‐sluggish reaction rates causing slab stagnation with re‐sharpened, deeply displaced 410s ( 100 km). Rheological contrasts modulate these kinetic effects by controlling slab geometry and residence time in the phase transition zone. These findings demonstrate that reaction rates strongly influence 410 structure in subduction zones, establishing the 410 as a potential seismological constraint on upper mantle kinetic processes, particularly in cold environments where disequilibrium effects are amplified.
